title: Coulomb-mediated interactions of charge-transfer excitons in TMD lateral heterostructures
authors: Kabyashree Sonowal, Daniel Erkensten, Ermin Malic, Roberto Rosati
abstract: Lateral heterostructures of transition-metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) host spatially separated charge-transfer (CT) excitons. While analogous to interlayer excitons in vertical TMD heterostructures, these interfacial excitons possess much larger in-plane dipoles of several nanometers and an additional center-of-mass quantization. Here, we study the mutual interactions between these highly dipolar CT excitons on a microscopic footing. Accounting for the dipolar and quantum exchange interactions, we evaluate the experimentally accessible density-dependent energy renormalization and predict a net energy blueshift of a few meV for bound CT excitons. Interestingly, for small dipole moments, the energy renormalization displays a quadratic dependence with respect to the dipole moment, in contrast to the linear dependence found in vertical TMD heterostructures. We show that spatial energy offset and temperature are the key tuning knobs for controlling the density-dependent excitonic response. Overall, our results contribute to a better microscopic understanding of CT excitons and their interactions in lateral TMD heterostructures.
summary: This work provides a microscopic theory for Coulomb interactions in CT excitons within TMD lateral heterostructures. It predicts a density-dependent energy blueshift, uniquely showing a quadratic dependence on the dipole moment. The study highlights that temperature and band offset are key external knobs for controlling this excitonic response.
main_problem: The study investigates the mutual Coulomb-mediated interactions and resulting density-dependent energy renormalization of charge-transfer (CT) excitons.
main_result: The energy renormalization is predicted to be a net blueshift, which exhibits a quadratic dependence on the dipole moment for small dipoles, contrasting with linear scaling in vertical heterostructures.
method: The authors employed a microscopic theoretical framework, using an equation-of-motion approach and modeling the interaction terms (dipole-dipole, bosonic, fermionic exchange) via Gaussian wavefunctions.
model_or_system: The system is based on lateral heterostructures (LHs) of TMDs, hosting spatially separated CT excitons with large in-plane dipoles and center-of-mass quantization. The model accounts for various interaction terms derived from the Coulomb interaction.
key_observables: Density-dependent energy shift ($\Delta E$), which is predicted to be a net blueshift.
important_parameters: Dipole moment ($d$), band offset ($\Delta E_v$), temperature ($T$), and exciton density ($n$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on decoupling the center-of-mass and relative motion ($\Psi(Qx, q) = \psi(Qx) ar{\phi}(q)$) and assumes an equilibrium, unscreened environment.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the schematic interactions (d-d, bos-ex, fer-ex), the dependence of energy shifts on band offset and dipole moment, and the non-monotonic temperature dependence of the energy shift.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the physical system and interaction terms to calculating the density dependence, then analyzing the role of the band offset, followed by a detailed investigation of temperature effects on the various interaction contributions.
why_interesting: The detailed microscopic treatment of dipolar interactions and the identification of tunable parameters like temperature and band offset make this highly relevant for understanding excitonic dynamics in engineered quantum materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic illustration showing charge transfer (CT) excitons at the interface of the MoSe2 −WSe2 lateral heterostructure as well as regular intralayer excitons far away from the interface. The 1D interface is chosen to be along the y direction. CT excitons interact via dipole-dipole (d-d) in- teraction, bosonic exchange (bos-ex), and fermionic exchange (fer-ex). (b) Formation of conduction (∆Ec) and valence band offsets (∆Ev) at opposite sides of the interface, where E0 W and E0 Mo are intrinsic single-particle band gaps of the constituent TMD monolayers.

FIG. 2. Density-dependent energy renormalization as a func- tion of 2D exciton densities at T = 0 K for two different band offsets: (a) ∆Ev = 215 meV, where bound CT exci- tons are present, and (b) ∆Ev = 0 meV corresponding to the monolayer exciton limit. Note that the direct term comprises the dipole-dipole interaction as well as the bosonic exchange, which contribute equally at vanishing temperature.

FIG. 3. (a) Exciton energy shifts in MoSe2-WSe2 LHs as function of the energy band offset. The schematics in the insets show intralayer excitons at low band offsets (with a delocalised COM wavefunction) and bound CT excitons at the interface (with a confined COM wavefunction) at higher band offsets. (b) Dipole moment of CT excitons as a function of the band offset. We consider T = 0 K and the exciton density is fixed to n = 1011 cm−2.

FIG. 4. (a) Dipole dependence of the Coulomb-induced en- ergy renormalization at T=0 K for a fixed density of n = 1011 cm−2 of CT excitons in LHs (main plot) and interlayer excitons in VHs (inset). The dipole moment of interlayer excitons is tuned by adjusting the hBN spacer thickness of the MoSe2-hBN-WSe2 heterostructure. The dashed lines de- note the energy shifts within the small-dipole approximation. Modification of energy shift in the hytpothetical cases of (b) delocalized COM (∆X = ∆X|∆Ev=0) and (c) tightly bound CT excitons in relative space (∆r = ∆r|∆Ev=0).
FIG. 5. Energy shift as a function of temperature for CT excitons at ∆Ev = 215 meV and at a fixed density of n = 1011cm−2.
